title: Eye, Ear, and Nasal Medication Administration
subtitle: The nurse instills eye, ear, and nasal medications correctly — drops into the conjunctival sac, room-temperature ear drops with the right pinna pull, and a blown nose before nasal sprays.

patient.name: Eye/Ear/Nasal Meds
patient.admitDate: The nurse administers ophthalmic, otic, and nasal medications using correct technique.

nursesNote: The nurse administers ophthalmic, otic, and nasal medications safely. Eye drops are placed into the lower conjunctival sac (never directly on the cornea) without touching the dropper to the eye or lashes; gentle pressure on the inner canthus for 30-60 seconds reduces systemic absorption, and when two drops are ordered the nurse waits about 5 minutes between them and gives drops before ointment. Ear drops are warmed to room temperature to avoid vertigo, and the pinna is pulled up and back in adults (down and back in children under 3) before instilling along the side of the canal, with the patient staying side-lying for a few minutes. Before a nasal spray the patient blows the nose and then sniffs gently during instillation. The nurse verifies the rights, performs hand hygiene, and avoids contaminating any dropper or tip.
providerOrders: Instill eye drops into the lower conjunctival sac without touching the eye
Apply pressure to the inner canthus after eye drops
Warm ear drops to room temperature before instilling
Pull the pinna up and back (adults) or down and back (young children)
Have the patient blow the nose before a nasal spray

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about these routes?

opts.text: Eye drops go into the lower conjunctival sac, not on the cornea
Pressure on the inner canthus reduces systemic absorption of eye drops
Ear drops should be warmed to room temperature before instilling
In adults, the pinna is pulled down and back for ear drops
The dropper tip should not touch the eye, lashes, or ear
The patient should blow the nose before using a nasal spray
Cold ear drops can cause dizziness or vertigo
In children under 3, the pinna is pulled down and back
When two eye drops are ordered, wait about 5 minutes between them
Eye ointment is applied before eye drops
The patient stays side-lying a few minutes after ear drops
Closing the eye gently (not squeezing) keeps the drop in place

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order the steps to instill eye drops

opts.text: Perform hand hygiene and verify the rights
Have the patient tilt the head back and look up
Pull down the lower lid to form a pocket
Drop the medication into the sac without touching the eye
Apply pressure to the inner corner and have the patient close the eye
Dropping eye medication into the lower conjunctival sac
Placing eye drops directly onto the cornea
Pulling the adult's pinna up and back for ear drops
Using cold ear drops straight from the refrigerator
Touching the dropper tip to the eyelashes
Applying pressure to the inner canthus after eye drops
Having the patient blow the nose before a nasal spray
Pulling a young child's pinna up and back

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: Order instilling ear drops in an adult
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Warm the drops to room temperature
Position the patient side-lying with the affected ear up
Pull the pinna up and back to straighten the canal
Instill the drops along the side of the canal
Have the patient stay side-lying for about 5 minutes
Instilling eye drops into the conjunctival sac
Touching the dropper to the eyelashes
Warming ear drops before use
Pulling an adult's pinna up and back
Squeezing the eyes tightly shut after drops
Having the patient lie still after ear drops
Instilling a second eye drop immediately on the first
Blowing the nose before a nasal spray
